comments.text: From the little Lean I can read, I see that the formalized statement still captures an older problem variant, which can be proved way too easily (see my post above). The new agreed-upon variant assumes that the intervals are non-overlapping and non-adjacent. I suppose that the current theorem statement theorem erdos_289 : (∀ᶠ k : ℕ in atTop, ∃ I : Fin k → Finset ℕ, (∀ i, 2 ≤ #(I i) ∧ ∃ a b, 0 < a ∧ I i = Finset.Icc a b) ∧ ∑ i, ∑ n ∈ I i, (n⁻¹ : ℚ) = 1) ↔ answer(sorry) := by sorry should be changed to, say, theorem erdos_289 : (∀ᶠ k : ℕ in atTop, ∃ I : Fin k → Finset ℕ, (∀ i, 2 ≤ #(I i) ∧ ∃ a b, 0 < a ∧ I i = Finset.Icc a b) ∧ (∀ i j, i ≠ j → ∀ x ∈ I i, ∀ y ∈ I j, x + 1 < y ∨ y + 1 < x) ∧ ∑ i, ∑ n ∈ I i, (n⁻¹ : ℚ) = 1) ↔ answer(sorry) := by sorry but I am (still) not confident enough with Lean to go and mess with the file at FormalConjectures. :-)

In the current formulation of this problem, the intervals are allowed to overlap and even repeat. This formulation is the one literally taken from [EG80] and the same one was formalized in Lean and emphasized in Dogmachine's comment. Let me present a very easy affirmative solution to this problem interpretation. However, afterwards I will explain that this is probably not what Erdős and Graham wanted to ask. From the following auxiliary result you will immediately realize what makes things easy in this interpretation of the problem. Lemma. If $M$ is a multiset with elements\[ z+1,z+2,z+3,z+4\in\mathbb{N} \]appearing with respective multiplicities $m_1,m_2,m_3,m_4$ that satisfy\[ m_2 \geq m_1, \ m_3 \geq m_4, \ m_1+m_4\geq \max\{m_2,m_3\}, \]then, for every\[ 0\leq u \leq m_1+m_4 -\max\{m_2,m_3\}, \]the multiset $M$ can be split into precisely $m_1+m_4-u$ integer-intervals of length $\geq2$. (The idea is to vary $u$ in applications.) For the proof write elements WLOG as $1,2,3,4$. We simply take $m_1-m_3+m_4-u$ times interval $\{1,2\}$, $m_1-m_2+m_4-u$ times interval $\{3,4\}$, $m_3-m_4$ times interval $\{1,2,3\}$, $m_2-m_1$ times interval $\{2,3,4\}$, $u$ times interval $\{1,2,3,4\}$. Now we turn back to the problem, which became much easier as we only need to find appropriate multisets that decompose $1$ into the corresponding unit fractions. It might still not be obvious how to do that, so I include the details. Take four different primes $p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\geq 10^{10}$. Find positive integers $a_1,\ldots,a_4$ and $b_1,\ldots,b_4$ such that \begin{align*} \frac{2}{10} & = \frac{a_1}{10p_1} + \frac{b_1}{p_1} + \frac{b_1}{p_1} + \frac{b_1}{p_1},\\ \frac{3}{10} & = \frac{b_2}{p_2} + \frac{a_2}{10p_2} + \frac{b_2}{p_2} + \frac{b_2}{p_2},\\ \frac{3}{10} & = \frac{b_3}{p_3} + \frac{b_3}{p_3} + \frac{a_3}{10p_3} + \frac{b_3}{p_3},\\ \frac{2}{10} & = \frac{b_4}{p_4} + \frac{b_4}{p_4} + \frac{b_4}{p_4} + \frac{a_4}{10p_4} \end{align*} and \begin{align*} & \Big|\frac{a_1}{10p_1}-\frac{2}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001, \ \Big|\frac{b_1}{p_1}-\frac{2}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001,\\ & \Big|\frac{a_2}{10p_2}-\frac{3}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001, \ \Big|\frac{b_2}{p_2}-\frac{3}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001,\\ & \Big|\frac{a_3}{10p_3}-\frac{3}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001, \ \Big|\frac{b_3}{p_3}-\frac{3}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001,\\ & \Big|\frac{a_4}{10p_4}-\frac{2}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001, \ \Big|\frac{b_4}{p_4}-\frac{2}{40}\Big| \leq 0.001. \end{align*} (We can say that the fractions on the left are split into $4$ almost equal parts.) The correct decomposition into multisets is obtained by taking the columns of the above splitting. Namely, denote\[ N=10p_1p_2p_3p_4. \]We will only describe the first multiset in detail. By the Chinese Remainder Theorem there exists $w\in\mathbb{N}$, $w\in[0,N)$ such that\[ 10p_1|w+1,\ p_2|w+2,\ p_3|w+3,\ p_4|w+4. \]For every $j\in\mathbb{N}$ we can then write \begin{align*} \frac{a_1}{10p_1} & = \frac{m_1(j)}{jN+w+1},\\ \frac{b_2}{p_2} & = \frac{m_2(j)}{jN+w+2},\\ \frac{b_3}{p_3} & = \frac{m_3(j)}{jN+w+3},\\ \frac{b_4}{p_4} & = \frac{m_4(j)}{jN+w+4}, \end{align*} where the numerators are positive integers. Proceeding similarly with the other three columns we obtain \begin{align*} 1 = & \sum_{l=1}^{4} \frac{m_l(j)}{jN+w+l} + \sum_{l=1}^{4} \frac{m'_l(j)}{jN+w'+l}\\ & + \sum_{l=1}^{4} \frac{m''_l(j)}{jN+w''+l} + \sum_{l=1}^{4} \frac{m'''_l(j)}{jN+w'''+l}, \end{align*} which is the splitting according to four multisets on consecutive $4$-tuples of integers. The first multiset is on $\{jN+w+l:1\leq l\leq 4\}$ and its multiplicities $m_l(j)$ satisfy conditions of the Lemma for sufficiently large $j$, because: $m_2(j)-m_1(j)$ differs from $jN/40$ by at most $0.002jN + O(1)$, $m_3(j)-m_4(j)$ differs from $jN/40$ by at most $0.002jN + O(1)$, $m_1(j)+m_4(j)-m_2(j)$ differs from $jN/40$ by at most $0.003jN + O(1)$, $m_1(j)+m_4(j)-m_3(j)$ differs from $jN/40$ by at most $0.003jN + O(1)$. The Lemma then guarantees its decomposition into intervals of length $\geq2$, where the total number of intervals is\[ (p_2p_3p_4a_1 + 10p_1p_2p_3b_4) j + \frac{w+1}{10p_1}a_1 + \frac{w+4}{p_4}b_4 - u, \]where $u$ is allowed to range over $[0,1000N)$ provided that $j$ is large enough. Proceeding like that with the other three multisets, we finally get\[ 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{n\in I_i}\frac{1}{n}, \]where the total number of intervals $k$ can be any number of the form\[ Cj + D - u \]for some concrete $C,D\in\mathbb{N}$, $C\leq 1000N$, sufficiently large $j\in\mathbb{N}$, and $u\in[0,1000N)$ arbitrary. These cover all sufficiently large positive integers and the proof is complete. Now, here is my doubt. In [EG80] on p.34, the problem statement is followed by an analogous representation of the number $2$ found by P. L. Montgomery (and independently also by D. Hickerson) in the solution to Monthly problem E2689 proposed by L.-S. Hahn: \begin{align*} 2 = & \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{7}}_{I_1} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{10}}_{I_2} \\ & + \underbrace{\frac{1}{17} + \frac{1}{18}}_{I_3}+ \underbrace{\frac{1}{34} + \frac{1}{35}}_{I_4}+ \underbrace{\frac{1}{84} + \frac{1}{85}}_{I_5}. \end{align*} It could be that Erdős and Graham were not able to find a single such representation of the number $1$, so they used the example with $2$ instead. Moreover, when one goes to look into the reference, one finds that the said problem was to represent just any integer as $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{n\in I_i}1/n$ for non-overlapping $I_i$ of length $\geq2$ (restated to match the notation here). In any case, this example hints that Erdős and Graham might have wanted the intervals $I_i$ to be non-overlapping. Moreover, maybe even adjacent intervals (like $\{4,5\}$ and $\{6,7\}$) are not allowed, since their total count ($=k$) is important. I would be in favor of changing the problem formulation to a nontrivial one (perhaps the most restrictive one), even though I am clueless on how to approach the non-overlapping case. There is plentitude of unit fractions summing to $1$, but not even nearly enough for some pigeonholing to group them into intervals. Some other variants of the above simple proof are in fact possible, for instance to also avoid adjacent intervals, but the same idea is not of much help if we want the intervals to be non-overlapping, or even just mutually different.
In this question, repeated intervals are allowed. However, in [ErGr80], It is pointed out that, in any potential solution, it is not possible for all intervals to be the same.
This is the dual question to [288]
